g.data-narration: Welcome! This lecture covers files in operating systems, their attributes, operations, and three key access methods.
A file is a logical collection of information stored on secondary storage like hard disks, persisting even when the computer is off.
g.text: A file is a logical collection of information stored on secondary storage
(hard disk, SSD). Data persists even when computer is powered off.
From OS perspective: a file is simply a sequence of bytes.

g.data-narration: Each file has attributes: name, identifier (inode), type, location pointer, size, protection permissions, and timestamps.
OS provides operations: create, write, read, seek (reposition pointer), delete, and truncate files.
Three main file access methods determine how records are accessed: Sequential, Direct, and Indexed Sequential.
g.text: The method by which records of a file are accessed determines performance.

g.data-narration: Sequential access processes data in order, one record after another, like reading a magnetic tape from start to finish.
g.text: Data is processed in order, one record after another. Like a magnetic tape -
must read through record 1, 2, 3... to reach record 100.

g.data-narration: Sequential operations: read next, write next, and rewind to reset the pointer to the beginning of the file.
Direct access allows reading/writing records in any arbitrary order. The file is viewed as numbered blocks like an array.
g.text: Records can be accessed in ANY order. File viewed as numbered blocks -
like an array. Can read block 14, then 53, then 7 without reading in between.

g.data-narration: Direct access operations include read n, write n, position to n, and seek() is crucial for jumping to any block.
Indexed Sequential Access Method (ISAM) combines sequential storage with an index for efficient direct access by key field.
g.text: A sophisticated hybrid! Builds an INDEX on top of sequentially stored data.
Search index → Follow pointer → Access record directly. Best of both worlds!

g.data-narration: ISAM works by creating a sorted index file with keys and pointers. Search the small index, then jump directly to the data record.
Sequential is simple but slow for random access. Direct is fast but needs fixed-length records. ISAM is fast for key searches but needs extra storage.
Real-world applications: Sequential for music streaming, Direct for SSDs and databases, ISAM for large customer databases.
Key takeaways: Files store data persistently with attributes. Three access methods trade-off speed, complexity, and storage overhead.
g.text: Secondary storage with attributes (name, size, permissions)
Sequential: Simple | Direct: Fast | ISAM: Best of both
Search small index → Jump to record via pointer

g.data-narration: Exam tips: Know definitions, contrast methods with a table, think practically about devices, and understand seek() operation importance.
